content.full_text:
OFFICE OF

COUNTY CLERK-RECORDER

REGISTRAR OF VOTERS
912 - 11th Street,
Room 100
Modesto, Ca. 95354

Telephone (209) 558-6313

KAREN L. MATHEWS, Clerk-Recorder

June 7, 1991


Mr. Peter McNamee
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Jim Jasper, Central California Irrigation District

Dear Mr. McNamee,

Enclosed is correspondence concerning the above named person who
is a Director of the Central California Irrigation District
(CCID). Mr. Jasper's area on the District is within Stanislaus
County. He last filed nomination papers for the November, 1991,
UDDL election and was appointed in lieu of an election because
there was no competition.

A survey was conducted in March, 1991, in order to confirm filing
responsibilities of the officeholders of school and special
districts in Stanislaus County. The CCID verbally advised that
its Board members receive compensation of $100 or more per month.

On April 8, 1991, specific written notification was sent to Mr.
Jasper regarding non-filing of his semi-annual report due
January 31, 1991. On April 23, 1991, the individual was referred
to the Stanislaus County District Attorney for a possible
violation. (The date of this letter was inadvertently misdated
as March 23, 1991.)

Ensuing correspondence and telephone conversations with Paul
Minasian, Attorney at Law, have not resolved the question of Mr.
Jasper's filing requirements. Our view is that having been
appointed in lieu of an election, Mr. Jasper serves as if elected
and has a filing requirement as an officeholder. (Wayne Imberi
of the Technical Assistance & Analysis Division has verbally
concurred with this view.) Mr. Minasian feels that since Mr.
Jasper never appeared on a ballot, he was never a candidate and
has no filing requirement per 1 FPPC Opinions 162.

RE:   Jim Jasper, CCID, June 7, 1991                           (2)

I am therefore referring this matter to you for an opinion on the
filing requirement of an officeholder who has been appointed in
lieu of an election, who receives compensation of $100 or more
per month.

                                          Sincerely,

                                          KAREN L. MATHEWS
                                          County Clerk-Recorder

                                          BY: ___________________
                                              Joyce Goudie
                                              Assistant Registrar of Voters

KLM/JG/nh
Enc

cc:  Paul Minasian, Attorney At Law
     Donald Stahl, District Attorney
     Jim Jasper, CCID
     Board of Directors, CCID
     Michael Krausnick, County Counsel

MINASIAN, MINASIAN, MINASIAN, SPRUANCE, BABER, MEITH & SOARES
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

PAUL R MINASIAN, INC.
(A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS)
JOHN CARLETON GRAY 1872-1913
WILLIAM M SPRUANCE, INC.
181 BIRD STREET
CARLETON GRAY 1893-1944
WILLIAM H. BABER III INC.
P.O BOX 1679
PAUL JACKSON MINASIAN 1933-1981
JEFFREY A MEITH
OROVILLE, CALIFORNIA 95965-1679
H. ANTHONY SOARES
(DAVID H. MINASIAN
MICHAEL V. SEXTON
TELEPHONE (916) 533-2885
OF COUNSEL
TELECOPIER (916) 533-0197
TIM O'LAUGHLIN

May 13, 1991


Joyce Goudie
Assistant Registrar of Voters
Stanislaus County Clerk-Recorder
912 - 11th Street, Room 100
Modesto, CA 95354

RE: Central California Irrigation District

Dear Ms. Goudie:

I appreciate your letter of May 9, 1991, even though it gave me some concern that there may have been some change in the law which made a party who filed for the position of Director under the Uniform District Elections law, a candidate for the purposes of Campaign Statement filing. Your letter was therefore a good opportunity for me to review the statute and the cases upon the matter.

Let me try to explain to you why our view of the law and that of the Fair Political Practices Commission, which is that Mr. Jasper does not have to file a campaign statement and is not subject to the requirements of Forms 470 or 490 since he was appointed to his position and has not been a candidate. As you know, the requirement for filing a campaign statement arises under Government Code Section 84200, et seq. The introductory sections to this portion of the Government Code define the word "candidate". Section 82007 states: "'Candidate'" means an individual who is listed on the ballot or who has qualified to have write-in votes on his or her behalf . . . "

As you know, when only one person files, there is no ballot prepared and the party is simply appointed. In short, the person is never a candidate.

When the Fair Political Practices Commission was first established, my father sat on an advisory commission established to deal with the special problems of special districts and I had a faint recollection that this subject had been resolved. In reviewing the Fair Political Practices Commission decisions, I found, in fact, that it was resolved in 1975 (the first year of the Commission's activities) at the request of the County Clerks of El Dorado and Fresno County. Commission's activities at the request of the County Clerks of El Dorado and Fresno County Commission opinion, 1 FPPC Opinions 162. As you can see, the opinion basically

[illegible handwritten notes at bottom of page]

CCID\GOUD5-12.LD1
PRM/ma
May 13, 1991
Page 2

examines the provisions of the Government Code and Elections Code under the Uniform District
Election law, and concludes that a person is not a candidate and that unless payments are made
by the person, or received by the person, the alternative requirement that a person may become
a candidate if he receives contributions or expends any funds, would not apply.

I include a copy of that opinion for your information and review.

Since Mr. Jasper was in fact appointed as the sole candidate and since he did not receive
or expend any amounts, it would appear that no campaign statement requirement applies. This
may be helpful to you in dealing with the other special districts within Stanislaus County.

Since you sent a copy of your letter to a number of people, I am taking the liberty of
sending a copy to those same persons. I am also asking that a copy go to Michael Krausnick,
County Counsel of Stanislaus County, as you may wish to discuss this with him.

Very truly yours,

MINASIAN, MINASIAN, MINASIAN,
SPRUANCE, BABER, MEITH & SOARES

By_______________________________
PAUL R. MINASIAN

PRM/ma

cc: Donald Stahl, District Attorney
Jim Jasper, CCID
Board of Directors, CCID
Michael H. Krausnick, County Counsel

1 FPPC OPINIONS 162

BEFORE THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

In the Matter of:

Opinion requested by
Carl A. Kelly
County Clerk
El Dorado County

H.L. Masini
County Clerk
Fresno County

)
)
)
)
)
)
)
)
)
)

No. 75-134
No. 75-171-A
October 23, 1975

BY THE COMMISSION: We have been asked the following
questions by Carl A. Kelly, County Clerk, El Dorado County,
and H.L. Masini, County Clerk, Fresno County:

(1) If a person files a nomination petition for
elective district office under the Uniform District Election
Law (Elections Code Sections 23500, et seq.) and such office
is vacant by appointment and no election is conducted, is
the person required to file any campaign statements under
the Political Reform Act?

(2) If a candidate is required to deposit funds with
the clerk's office for the payment of costs to be incurred
in the printing of the candidate's qualification statement,
is the deposit, although later refunded, reportable expen-
diture under Government Code Section 84200?

CONCLUSION

(1) A person seeking elective office under the Uniform
District Election Law who is subsequently appointed to office
need not file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code
Sections 84200, et seq., unless funds are raised or expended
by him or in his behalf with a view to bringing about his
by him or in his behalf with a view to bringing about his
nomination or election to any elective office.

2/78

No. 75-134
No. 75-171-A
Page Two

(2) The deposit of money with the clerk's office is
an advance payment for services to be rendered and must be
reported pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq.
unless the refund is received by the candidate prior to the
closing date for the filing of his first campaign statement.

ANALYSIS

(1) Elections Code Section 21512 establishes the
procedure for filing nomination petitions for an elective
district office. If 60 days prior to an election only one
person has been nominated for a district office, a super-
vising authority must appoint the nominee to office.1/

Government Code Sections 84200, et seq.2/ require
candidates to file periodic campaign statements. The term
"candidate" is defined in Section 82007:

Candidate means an individual who is listed on
the ballot ... or who receives a contribution
or makes an expenditure or gives his consent
for any other person to receive a contribution
or makes an expenditure with a view to bringing
about his nomination or election to any elective
office ....

Thus, there are two provisions of Section 82007 which
may cause a prospective appointee to be a candidate. First,
if a person is "listed on the ballot" he is a candidate. In
most instances, anyone who has qualified to have his name
listed on the ballot will in fact have his name so listed.
Therefore, the general rule is that when a person has his
name "listed on the ballot" for the ballot he is "listed on the ballot" for the
purposes of Section 82007. However, if a person quali-
fies his name for the ballot, he will not be a candidate
unless by operation of Section 82007 if, by operation of law,
his name nevertheless does not actually appear on the ballot.
Accordingly, the prospective appointees in the instant case
are not "candidates" merely because of the fact that they
qualified their names for the ballot.

1/ The appointment, however, may not actually occur until
several weeks after the date of the general election.

2/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

4/78

No. 75-134
No. 75-171-A
Page Three

1 FPPC OPINIONS 164

A second means of becoming a candidate within the
meaning of Section §2007 is if a person receives any con-
tributions or expends any funds, or authorizes anyone else
to do so, with a view to bringing about his nomination or
election to any elective office. Payments for filing fees,
pamphlets, newspapers, etc. are "expenditures" under
Section §2025. Therefore, payments like these, which are
directed with a view toward bringing about a person's nom-
ination or election, will cause that person to be a candidate.
If a prospective appointee is a candidate, he must file cam-
paign statements or a verified statement that not more than
$200 has been received or expended on behalf of his candidacy.
Section §4212.

(2) A candidate for an elective office
may submit information for inclusion in a candidate quali-
fication booklet pursuant to Section 2140.5, which is
mailed, along with a sample ballot, to each voter. Elec-
tions Code Section 10012.5. The clerk's office bills each
candidate for his pro rata share of the cost of producing
the candidate qualification booklet. Elections Code Sec-
tion 10012.5. To simplify this billing procedure, the
clerk's office requires each candidate to post a deposit to
cover his share of the cost. If, however, the clerk
faces no opposition 60 days prior to the election, he will be
appointed to office by the supervising authority and, in such
a case, his qualification statement deposit will be refunded.

The deposit posted by the candidate is an advance pay-
ment to the clerk's office for costs to be incurred in the
printing of the candidate's qualification statement. Since
the deposit is a payment, it is an expenditure within the
meaning of Section §2025. Moreover, an expenditure occurs
when the payment is made and must be reported pursuant to
Sections §4200, et seq. Therefore, the entire deposit
with expenditure, even though a subsequent billing may re-
quire additional payment or result in a partial refund. Ho-
wever, if a refund is received by the candidate prior to the
60-day period before the filing of his first campaign statement,
the refunded portion of the deposit need not be reported
pursuant to Sections §4200, et seq.


Approved by the Commission on October 23, 1975.
Concurring: Brosnahan, Miller and Lowenstein. Commissioners
Carpenter and Waters were absent.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

2/76

sections.conclusion:
(1) A person seeking elective office under the Uniform District Election Law who is subsequently appointed to office need not file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. unless funds are received or expended by him or in his behalf with a view to bringing about his nomination or election to any elective office.

(2) The deposit of money with the clerk's office is an advance payment for services to be rendered and must be reported pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. unless the refund is received by the candidate prior to the closing date for the filing of his first campaign statement.
sections.analysis:
(1) Elections Code Section 23512 establishes the procedure for filing nomination petitions for an elective district office. If 60 days prior to an election only one person has been nominated for a district office, the supervising authority must appoint the nominee to office.1/

Government Code Sections 84200, et seq.2/ require candidates to file periodic campaign statements. The term "candidate" is defined in Section 82007:

Candidate means an individual who is listed on the ballot ... or who receives a contribution or makes an expenditure or gives his consent for any other person to receive a contribution or make an expenditure with a view to bringing about his nomination or election to any elective office ....

Thus, there are two provisions of Section 82007 which may cause a prospective appointee to be a candidate. First, if a person is "listed on the ballot" he is a candidate. In most instances, anyone who has qualified to have his name listed on the ballot will in fact have his name so listed. Therefore, the general rule is that when a person qualifies his name for the ballot he is "listed on the ballot" for the purposes of Section 82007. However, even if a person qualifies his name for the ballot, he will not be a candidate within the meaning of Section 82007 if, by operation of law, his name nevertheless does not actually appear on the ballot. Accordingly, the prospective appointees in the instant case are not "candidates" merely because of the fact that they qualified their names for the ballot.

1/
The appointment, however, may not actually occur until several weeks after the date of the general election.

2/
All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted.

A second means of becoming a candidate within the meaning of Section 82007 is if a person receives any contributions or expends any funds, or authorizes anyone else to do so, with a view to bringing about his nomination or election to any elective office. Payments for filing fees, pamphlets, newsletters, campaign supplies, etc., are "expenditures." Section 82025. Therefore, payments like these, when made with a view toward bringing about a person's nomination or election, will cause that person to be a candidate. If a prospective appointee is a candidate, he must file campaign statements or a verified statement that not more than $200 has been received or expended on behalf of his candidacy. Section 84212.

(2) A candidate for election to a district office may submit information for inclusion in a candidate qualification booklet, Elections Code Section 2340.5, which is mailed along with a sample ballot, to each voter. Elections Code Section 10012.5. The clerk's office bills each candidate for his pro rata share of the cost of producing the candidate qualification booklet. Elections Code Section 10012.5. To simplify this billing procedure, the clerk's office requires each candidate to post a deposit to cover his share of the cost. If, however, the candidate faces no opposition 60 days prior to the election, he will be appointed to office by the supervising authority and, in such a case, his qualification statement deposit will be refunded.

The deposit posted by the candidate is an advance payment to the clerk's office for costs to be incurred in the printing of the candidate's qualification statement. Since the deposit is a payment, it is an expenditure within the meaning of Section 32025. Moreover, an expenditure occurs when the payment is made and must be reported pursuant to Sections 84200, et seq. Therefore, the deposit is a reportable expenditure, even though a subsequent billing may require additional payment or provide a partial refund. However, if a refund is received by the candidate prior to the closing date for the filing of his first campaign statement, the refunded portion of the deposit need not be reported pursuant to Sections 84200, et seq.

Approved by the Commission on October 23, 1975.
Concurring: Brosnahan, Miller and Lowenstein. Commissioners Carpenter and Waters were absent.

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: (1) A person seeking elective office under the Uniform District Election Law who is subsequently appointed to office need not file campaign statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. unless funds are received or expended by him or in his behalf with a view to bringing about his nomination or election to any elective office.

(2) The deposit of money with the clerk's office is an advance payment for services to be rendered and must be reported pursuant to Government Code Sections 84200, et seq. unless the refund is received by the candidate prior to the closing date for the filing of his first campaign statement.
